description: Key terms from Anderson's Dictionary of Law (1889) - municipal law, public officers, and procurement

terms.definition: A member of the governing body of a municipal corporation; a legislative officer of a city or borough. The board of aldermen typically exercises general legislative authority over municipal affairs.
The governing body of a county in many states; a board of elected officers charged with the general administration and legislation of county affairs including appropriations and contracts.

An incorporated municipal unit with a charter from the state; an incorporated town or village. In Pennsylvania and New Jersey, the name given to an incorporated municipality ranking between a village and a city.

A municipal corporation having a charter granted by the legislature, with defined powers of local government over a compact area. Cities are distinguished from towns and villages by population and governmental complexity.

The legal officer of a city who advises the municipal government, represents it in legal proceedings, and reviews contracts and ordinances for legal sufficiency; also called city solicitor or corporation counsel.
The legislative body of a city government; the elected representatives of the city who exercise legislative power, adopt ordinances, authorize expenditures, and approve contracts.
The municipal officer charged with the receipt, safe-keeping, and disbursement of public funds; responsible for maintaining accounts of revenues and expenditures and reporting to the governing body.

The body of non-elected government employees selected by examination or merit rather than patronage; the system of employment in government positions based on qualifications rather than political affiliation.

The governmental officer charged with auditing public accounts, examining the accounts of other officers, and ensuring that expenditures conform to appropriations; the chief financial accountability officer.

The largest political subdivision of a state; a territorial division of a state organized for local government purposes with defined powers of taxation, road maintenance, law enforcement, and judicial administration.
The officer who examines and certifies public accounts; responsible for auditing the accounts of county officers and ensuring the accuracy and legality of expenditures from public funds.
The officer who maintains the official records of the county, including deeds, mortgages, court records, and official proceedings; serves as secretary to the county governing board.
The inherent power of the sovereign to take private property for public use upon payment of just compensation; the right of the state to assert dominion over private property for a public purpose.
A municipal bond issued to finance the construction of public improvements such as streets, sidewalks, sewers, or public buildings; typically repaid from special assessments or general revenues.

A public officer authorized and required to examine objects or operations to ensure compliance with law or regulations; appointed to examine and report on the condition of public works, buildings, weights and measures, or business operations.
The imposition of a tax or assessment; the act of imposing and collecting a tax; also, the seizure of property pursuant to an execution. A tax levy is the formal act imposing the rate of taxation.

Permission granted by a competent authority to do an act which without such permission would be illegal; in municipal law, a grant of permission to engage in a trade, occupation, or activity within the jurisdiction.

A debt security issued by a state or local government to finance capital expenditures such as public buildings, highways, and school construction; interest is generally exempt from federal income taxation.

A public body politic created by the legislature out of the inhabitants of a particular locality, invested with subordinate powers of legislation, and a separate legal existence for purposes of local government.
The total indebtedness of a municipal corporation outstanding at a given time; subject to constitutional and statutory debt limits restricting the total indebtedness municipalities may incur.
A person holding an office in a municipal corporation, either by election, appointment, or otherwise; one who exercises the public functions of a municipal government under authority of law.
A government contract awarded to a vendor without competitive solicitation; generally disfavored and subject to strict justification requirements to prevent favoritism and ensure best value.

A bond given by a public officer as security for the faithful performance of his duties; a surety instrument protecting the public against loss caused by the officer's default, misconduct, or negligence.

A governmental body created by municipal charter or statute to manage public parks, recreation areas, and related facilities; may have authority to acquire land and enter contracts for park improvements.

The inherent authority of the state to enact reasonable laws and regulations to protect the public health, safety, morals, and general welfare; the most fundamental exercise of governmental authority over private conduct.
A tax levied on real and personal property based on its assessed value; the primary revenue source for local governments, typically assessed and collected by county or municipal officers.
Money belonging to the government held for public purposes; trust moneys in the custody of a public officer; funds raised by taxation or other public means for the support of governmental functions.

A position of trust, authority, and duty created and given existence by law; an employment on behalf of the public in some matter of governmental functions; a position under government imposing public duties.

Property owned by a government or one of its agencies for public use and benefit; property held in trust for the citizens by the government that may not be alienated without legislative authorization.

The acquisition of goods, services, or works by a government entity using public funds; subject to competitive bidding requirements and other regulations designed to ensure fair competition and best value.

An officer appointed by the court to receive and preserve the property or funds in litigation pending final disposition; an indifferent person between the parties appointed by the court to hold property.
A public officer who maintains and preserves official records; particularly, the officer charged with recording instruments relating to real property such as deeds, mortgages, and liens.
The termination of a public officer's service before the natural expiration of the term; may be accomplished by impeachment, recall, judicial proceedings, or executive action depending on the office.

The body created by law to govern a public school district; vested with authority to levy taxes for school purposes, employ teachers, manage school property, and enter contracts for school facilities.
The chief law enforcement officer of a county; an officer elected or appointed to preserve the peace, execute process, and perform other duties prescribed by law within the county.

A contract awarded to a single contractor without competition, justified by unique expertise, proprietary rights, emergency, or other circumstances making competition impracticable; requires specific justification.
A charge levied on property owners who benefit from a local improvement such as a street, sewer, or sidewalk; differs from a general tax in that it is proportionate to the benefit received.
The public officer responsible for collecting taxes; may receive and account for taxes levied by the municipality or county, issuing receipts and remitting collections to the treasurer.
The formal act by which a legislative body imposes the rate of taxation upon persons or property subject to tax; the act of fixing the rate and directing the assessment and collection of taxes.

An incorporated municipality ranking below a city; a political subdivision of a county with defined powers of local self-government granted by state law, including authority to levy taxes and adopt ordinances.
A governmental and geographic subdivision of a county in many states; an area of land six miles square established by federal land surveys; a body politic organized for local government purposes.

The officer of a government, corporation, or association charged with receiving, safely keeping, and disbursing public funds; accountable for all moneys received and obligated to disburse only on lawful authority.

The smallest form of incorporated municipality; a public corporation with limited powers of local government over a small community, organized by charter or statute with authority to levy taxes and adopt ordinances.
A governmental body created to manage public water systems, water supply, and related infrastructure; may have authority to levy assessments, contract for improvements, and regulate water usage.

The division of a municipality into zones or districts and the regulation of land use within those districts; the exercise of police power to regulate the use of private property for the public welfare.

A bid submitted in response to a public solicitation for proposals, subject to the requirement of public opening and award to the lowest responsible bidder; the mechanism for ensuring fair competition in public procurement.
The formal act of accepting a bid or proposal and creating a binding government contract; the official notification to a bidder that its proposal has been accepted and a contract is formed.

An agreement for the construction, repair, or maintenance of public infrastructure entered into by a governmental body; subject to competitive bidding requirements, prevailing wage laws, and performance bond requirements.

A public officer charged with the receipt, safekeeping, and disbursement of public funds; responsible for maintaining financial records, preparing financial reports, and ensuring compliance with appropriation law.

A statute requiring governmental bodies to conduct their deliberations and decisions in public; prohibiting secret meetings except for specified executive session purposes such as personnel matters and litigation.

Official records required by law to be kept, or necessarily kept in the discharge of a duty imposed by law; records of governmental proceedings which are open to public inspection under public records laws.
A local legislative enactment authorizing the expenditure of public funds for specified purposes during a fiscal year; the operative budget legislation of a municipal corporation.
